## Summary
The CMB Cold Spot is a statistically anomalous region approximately 10° across in the southern sky, significantly colder than expected from standard ΛCDM predictions. Its origin remains debated between a supervoid explanation via the integrated Sachs-Wolfe (ISW) effect and a primordial origin.

## Evidence
The Cold Spot was first identified in WMAP data and confirmed by Planck with a p-value of ~0.01 against Gaussian random fields. Galaxy surveys reveal an underdensity (supervoid) at z ≈ 0.15 aligned with the Cold Spot, though its depth and extent remain uncertain. The ISW signal from known voids accounts for only 10–20% of the observed temperature decrement.

## Significance
If the Cold Spot is primordial, it could indicate non-Gaussian initial conditions, topological defects, or a signature of bubble collisions in eternal inflation. If it is a supervoid artifact, it challenges our understanding of structure formation and void profiles at low redshift.
